title: Localization of a quantum particle in a classical one-component plasma. II. Dynamic Disorder and Temporal Decorrelation
abstract: We extend the static theory of disorder-induced exponential decay of the averaged Green function of a quantum charged particle in a classical one-component plasma to the dynamic regime by incorporating the temporal evolution of the ionic density fluctuations within the random phase approximation. The dynamic potential correlator is derived from the fluctuation-dissipation theorem and the Kramers--Kronig relations. Within the eikonal (straight-line) approximation, the effective disorder strength is expressed through the dielectric function of the ion plasma. For particles moving faster than the ion thermal speed, the static Coulomb logarithm is recovered, with the large-distance cutoff replaced by the dynamic scale $v/ω_{pi}$. For slow particles, the Coulomb logarithm disappears completely and the disorder strength becomes proportional to the velocity, leading to a fundamentally different scaling of the localization length. In particular, the strong-disorder length diverges as $k^{-1/3}$ for $v\ll v_{\mathrm{th}}$, whereas it saturates in the static limit, indicating that ultra-slow particles are not exponentially localized in a dynamic plasma. A crossover between the quasi-static and dynamic regimes occurs when the particle speed becomes comparable to the ion thermal speed.
summary: This paper investigates how the movement of ions in a plasma affects the localization of a quantum particle. It demonstrates that while fast particles behave according to static theory, ultra-slow particles experience a suppression of disorder that prevents exponential localization.
main_problem: Extending the theory of Anderson localization from a static random potential to a dynamic, time-dependent potential caused by ionic density fluctuations in a plasma.
main_result: The study finds that for slow particles, temporal decorrelation suppresses the Coulomb logarithm and leads to a scaling of the localization length where $\ell \propto k^{-1/3}$, implying that ultra-slow particles are not exponentially localized.
method: The author uses a path-integral formalism combined with the eikonal approximation, the Random Phase Approximation (RPA), and the fluctuation-dissipation theorem to derive the decay of the Green function.
model_or_system: A non-relativistic quantum particle with charge $q_0$ and mass $m$ moving in a classical one-component plasma (OCPS) of mobile ions, where the potential is a Gaussian random field generated by ionic density fluctuations.
key_observables: Averaged retarded Green function, localization length ($\ell$), decay rate ($\Gamma$), and effective dynamic disorder strength ($G_{dyn}$).
important_parameters: Particle velocity ($v$) vs. ion thermal speed ($v_{th}$), Debye screening length ($\kappa^{-1}$), and ion plasma frequency ($\omega_{pi}$).
main_assumptions: The eikonal (straight-line) approximation is used, neglecting quantum fluctuations of the path, and the plasma is treated within the Random Phase Approximation (RPA).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the dynamic problem, establishes the mathematical framework using path integrals and RPA, analyzes the two velocity regimes (fast vs. slow), and discusses the physical implications and scaling laws.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental look at how environmental dynamics (temporal decorrelation) can destroy localization, which is highly relevant to understanding decoherence and transport in many-body open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
